ICU Team Update — August 2026
Midland Metropolitan University Hospital

ICU Team Update

August 2026 • Critical Care
🔬
Research Corner
Trials • Publications • Recruitment

A big milestone for GenOMICC this month, plus an important safety point for anyone using Smart Care weaning — please read that one carefully before your next shift.

GenOMICC — June Recruitment 13 patients recruited in June, from 34 screened. Thank you to everyone involved in screening and consent — fifty patients recruited across 2026 is a fantastic team effort.
⚠️ Smart Care Weaning — RELEASE Trial Interaction Smart Care weaning will soon be introduced on ICU to support patients preparing for extubation — it automatically adjusts Pressure Support according to a patient's respiratory drive. Smart Care must not be used for patients randomised to the APRV arm of the RELEASE Trial, as automatic ventilator adjustments may conflict with the trial protocol. Please check trial allocation before starting Smart Care, and if you're unsure whether a patient is enrolled or whether Smart Care is appropriate, speak to the research team or a senior clinician before proceeding.
📋 Help Improve APRV Resources We're inviting all ICU staff to complete a short, anonymous survey to help improve APRV education and resource accessibility across SWB Trust. It takes about 2 minutes. Take the survey →

Watch: About the RELEASE trial

🌟
Team News
People • Celebrations • Farewells
🏆 SWB Trust Star Awards With more than 550 nominations across 17 categories, this year's Star Awards celebrated exceptional work across the Trust that often goes unnoticed by the wider organisation. Two brilliant wins for us: the Patient Choice Award went to the ICU Outreach Team, following a moving nomination from a patient's family who praised the team's expert, compassionate and reassuring care — and Sophie Whiles, Occupational Therapy Lead for Critical Care and Surgeries, won Clinical Unsung Hero. Huge congratulations to both.
The team on stage at the SWB Trust Star Awards.
A Family's Thank You Heavenly and her mum Kelly were delighted to hear their nomination had resulted in the Outreach Team winning the Patient Choice Award. Kelly said: "There are so many wonderful people within the Trust — it is those that make the perfect team. I don't think we appreciate just how much you all work together for us until we need you. Over the years Heavenly has been looked after by so many, but especially in recent times the Outreach Team usually have to step in — either supporting the ward she stays on, or because I feel she's taking a turn for the worse, or sometimes something isn't quite right and fresh eyes might be worth casting. I'm so proud of our NHS and I thank each and every one of you for taking the time to care for Heav and treat her with such kindness." Kelly and Heavenly were sorry they couldn't be there in person to present the award.
👶 Congratulations Lauren Lauren Price, Advanced Physiotherapist, has safely delivered baby Archie. Congratulations to the whole family!
📚 ACCP-Supervised Neurophysiology Placement During June, our ACCPs guided first-year trainees on the Scientist Training Programme within Neurophysiology, exploring the contribution of clinical assessment and investigation to patient care, and the roles and relationships of the multidisciplinary team involved. A great opportunity to forge links with the neurophysiology team who frequently attend ICU. Big thank you to Lucy and Emma (ACCPs), who went "above and beyond" — the trainees fed back that they took the time to explain things and relate them back to Neurophysiology. The ACCP team look forward to supporting more trainees in future.
Farewell to Megan — three years on ICU.
👋 Farewell Megan We're sorry to see Megan Farrington leave after three years on ICU. She wanted to thank the team for being amazing and for supporting her throughout her time here. Good luck, Megan — you'll be missed.
↑ Back to top
🏥
Clinical Updates
Protocols • Guidelines • Weaning
Smart Care and Tracheostomy Patients Smart Care is an established tool to support weaning from mechanical ventilation in appropriately selected patients. Recent MDT discussion highlighted the importance of patient selection, particularly in patients with tracheostomies and those anticipated to require a prolonged or complex weaning course. In these patients, an individualised weaning strategy may be more appropriate than an automated weaning protocol. Please ensure the agreed weaning plan is clearly documented and communicated within the MDT.
Smart Care and RECO Weans A quick reminder: SmartCare is not suitable for patients on a RECO wean.
Extubation Readiness — Spontaneous Breathing Trials A successful spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) should form part of the assessment of extubation readiness and should be clearly documented in the medical record. Where clinically appropriate, SBTs are typically performed on minimal ventilatory support (PEEP 5 or Pressure Support 5/5). As outlined in the Critical Care Extubation Checklist, SmartCare should be used to support the weaning process unless a documented clinical reason to opt out is recorded. Where SmartCare is used, the ventilator may display an "SBT Passed" prompt following successful completion of an objective assessment — this can provide supporting evidence that an SBT has been completed, and should be documented accordingly. Extubation decisions should always incorporate the overall clinical picture: airway protection, cough strength, secretion burden, neurological status, haemodynamic stability, and anticipated post-extubation support.
📋 Did You Know? — PREPARE-II Trial A fluid bolus given to an unstable patient during rapid sequence induction makes no difference to the rate of cardiovascular collapse, according to the PREPARE-II trial. The comparator wasn't a fully sealed face mask, and around 70% of patients were excluded (unable to tolerate NIV pre-oxygenation), so the result should be read with that caveat in mind. Practical takeaway for our unit: don't rely on a fluid bolus alone to prevent peri-induction collapse in unstable patients — a positive-pressure pre-oxygenation technique looks more likely to help. Full summary →

🛡️
Safety & Quality
Audit • Governance • Learning
Discharges to Ward — Inform Outreach Please remember to bleep Outreach on 6000, or speak directly to whoever is covering Outreach, whenever a patient is discharged from ICU to the ward. This is a good opportunity to hand over anything that needs follow-up. Outreach attend safety brief so they know about planned discharges, but there have been a few recent occasions where we haven't told them when the patient actually leaves — this delays their review, so please let them know at the time. Remember to complete a set of observations before transfer (Assessments → Fluid Balance → Adult Quick View → Vital Signs), so a NEWS score and Patient Wellness Score are generated. This applies to every patient, and the responsibility sits with the bedside nurse.
ICU Micro Round — Quiet Period There's a long-standing agreed quiet period on ICU during the afternoons, which is when the ICU Micro Round often takes place. If there's no need to review a patient face-to-face, please convene off Zone 1 or Zone 3 — the seminar room or Zone 2 work well. There's no barrier to urgent patient review if needed, but in practice this isn't how the micro ward rounds are usually run. Apologies if this hasn't been clear to everyone — it's been part of the ICU day for many years, and being asked to relocate isn't a reflection on the value of what you're doing.
⚠️ Are We Actually Writing "ARDS" in the Notes? A new global definition of ARDS (now including non-intubated patients, and SpO2-based criteria for resource-limited settings) and updated management guidelines were presented at this year's Intensive Care Society State Of The Art meeting — and we've already introduced steroids for ARDS patients at SWB. Worth asking ourselves: how often are we actually documenting "ARDS" as an active diagnosis in the notes? If it isn't written down as the working diagnosis, staff — particularly residents — may not trigger the relevant treatment bundle, including the newly introduced steroid pathway. Worth an informal audit of how consistently ARDS is captured as an active problem, alongside a reminder of the diagnostic criteria and current steroid protocol.
Documentation & Auditing Please ensure entries in patient records are accurate, contemporaneous, and succinct — minimise cut and paste. If you make a decision, practical, ethical, or otherwise, please "show your working" and write it down. Separately, if you're undertaking an audit on ICU, please let Balaji Ramamurthy (Audit Lead) know by email so it can be added to the audit database and included at the ICU Clinical Governance Meeting — audit topic, aims and objectives, methodology, expected end date/presentation date, and whether a re-audit is planned. If you've already started an audit and haven't shared these details yet, please do so when convenient.
⚠️ Sharp Bins — Check Assembly We've had sharp bins arrive not assembled correctly (e.g. lids not locking). Please be extra vigilant when getting a new sharp bin from the equipment room and ensure it's assembled properly. If faulty, please complete an IRI and leave it for housekeeping to report.
↑ Back to top
⚙️
Operational Matters
Equipment • Contacts • Process
Baton Phones — Primary Contact Method Baton phones are the primary mode of contact for the nurse in charge, zone leaders, registrar-tier doctor, SHOs and ACCPs. We don't support or encourage the use of personal phones for this. Please update your own contacts with the numbers below, and if you're carrying a baton phone, it needs to be with you, charged, and turned on.
Thursday & Friday Mornings — Educational Events ICU has educational events at 0830h on Thursday and Friday mornings in the seminar room — all welcome. Thursday is teaching, Friday is journal club. On these two days, the ward round starts at 1000h, and the tactical safety brief moves to 0930h.
New Interpreter Service — Training Video Please watch the training video for the new interpreter service, launching soon. Once a WOW (workstation on wheels) has been set up for this purpose, it will be stored in Zone 2 and used only for interpreting. Watch the training video →
Infinity III Feeding Pumps — Coming Soon All Nutricia feeding pumps will be changing from Infinity II to Infinity III across the hospital. They'll be rolled out once at least 80% of nurses hospital-wide have completed training (mandatory training on Centric Cortex). Further information has been shared with senior nursing staff and is on staff comms. Questions — speak to the dietitians or nutrition nurses.
Trust Pulse Survey Please complete the pulse survey — it's anonymous, and it's used to tell the Trust how its employees are feeling. Take the survey →
Newsletter Submissions If you've got something for next month's newsletter, please use the submission form rather than emailing or WhatsApping it directly — it makes life much easier on our end.
📜 ICUsteps The intensive care patient and family support charity. QR codes are available at the bedside on admission. icusteps.org →
Mandatory Training Please check that your mandatory training is current. If anything has lapsed, complete it promptly.

👀
Looking Ahead
Coming Up • Jon's Pick
🎉 FICM is Now CICM FICM officially became the College of Intensive Care Medicine (CICM) on 1 July 2026 — after 15 years as a faculty of eight parent colleges, ICM is now an independent Royal College in its own right. It won't change anything day-to-day for how we practise, but it does mean ICM has "grown up" and now holds the same status as many of the other mainline specialties.
External CPD • Save the Date
EEG in the ICU — 1-Day Meeting
Hosted by the International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE), British Branch • King's College Hospital
A one-day study day on EEG in the ICU, returning for another year — well worth a look if you're interested in neuromonitoring on critical care.

Date: Thursday 11th March 2027
Time: 09:15–17:00
Register →
ICU M&M — Save the Dates The next ICU Mortality & Morbidity meeting is Thursday 24th September (1530–1730), followed by Wednesday 18th November (1530–1730). All meetings are in the ICU seminar room, F2F only (not over Teams). Please make every effort to attend — it's part of QIHD, so if you're expected at that, you're expected at this too. If you'd like to present — observed themes, interesting cases, reflections, data and more — contact kdonnelly2@nhs.net.
🌊 Work Social — Cliff Lakes Aqua Park, Saturday 5th September Still on and still going strong — we're heading to Cliff Lakes aqua park on Saturday 5th September. Family and friends welcome; we've been before and it was great fun! Add your name (and number of guests) to the sign-up sheets on the unit; expect around £20–25 per person, depending on numbers.
🎃 Save the Date — Halloween Work Party, Saturday 24th October Also still on: our Halloween Work Party lands on Saturday 24th October at Thimblemill Recreation and Entertainment Centre. DJ and food to be confirmed once numbers are in, but one thing's non-negotiable: full fancy dress is a must!
